raw_response: {"rating": -1.8, "lean": "Lean Left", "explanation": "This is a news report/interview. The author's framing leans left by foregrounding DOJ findings of systemic civil-rights violations by police and curating related links and section headers emphasizing police misconduct and racial disparities. The consistent but moderate tilt warrants a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: 1) The headline centers the DOJ accusation (“The DOJ says the Louisville police routinely violated civil rights”), foregrounding systemic rights violations by police — a theme aligned with left-leaning concerns; 2) The author’s prose focuses on reform mechanisms (“legally enforceable… consent decree” and “36 improvements” in training, oversight, documentation), emphasizing government-driven remedies consistent with left preferences; 3) Section headers frame the conversation around systemic racial disparities (“On whether he thinks Louisville’s Black residents… have fundamentally different experiences with the police”), steering readers toward a civil-rights lens; 4) The curation of embedded link headlines reinforces a critical view of policing institutions: “A former cop pleads guilty to falsifying warrant…,” “Police are ‘shielded’ from repercussions of their abuse,” and “As state-run police expand into Jackson… Others see racism”; 5) No balancing perspectives from police unions, rank-and-file officers, or critics of DOJ consent decrees are included in the author’s own framing, while the sole interviewee (the mayor) is aligned with reforms, producing a one-sided structural emphasis."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report/interview. The author's framing leans left by foregrounding DOJ findings of systemic civil-rights violations by police and curating related links and section headers emphasizing police misconduct and racial disparities. The consistent but moderate tilt warrants a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: 1) The headline centers the DOJ accusation (“The DOJ says the Louisville police routinely violated civil rights”), foregrounding systemic rights violations by police — a theme aligned with left-leaning concerns; 2) The author’s prose focuses on reform mechanisms (“legally enforceable… consent decree” and “36 improvements” in training, oversight, documentation), emphasizing government-driven remedies consistent with left preferences; 3) Section headers frame the conversation around systemic racial disparities (“On whether he thinks Louisville’s Black residents… have fundamentally different experiences with the police”), steering readers toward a civil-rights lens; 4) The curation of embedded link headlines reinforces a critical view of policing institutions: “A former cop pleads guilty to falsifying warrant…,” “Police are ‘shielded’ from repercussions of their abuse,” and “As state-run police expand into Jackson… Others see racism”; 5) No balancing perspectives from police unions, rank-and-file officers, or critics of DOJ consent decrees are included in the author’s own framing, while the sole interviewee (the mayor) is aligned with reforms, producing a one-sided structural emphasis.

explanation: This is a news report/interview. The author's framing leans left by foregrounding DOJ findings of systemic civil-rights violations by police and curating related links and section headers emphasizing police misconduct and racial disparities. The consistent but moderate tilt warrants a Lean Left rating. Detailed Analysis: 1) The headline centers the DOJ accusation (“The DOJ says the Louisville police routinely violated civil rights”), foregrounding systemic rights violations by police — a theme aligned with left-leaning concerns; 2) The author’s prose focuses on reform mechanisms (“legally enforceable… consent decree” and “36 improvements” in training, oversight, documentation), emphasizing government-driven remedies consistent with left preferences; 3) Section headers frame the conversation around systemic racial disparities (“On whether he thinks Louisville’s Black residents… have fundamentally different experiences with the police”), steering readers toward a civil-rights lens; 4) The curation of embedded link headlines reinforces a critical view of policing institutions: “A former cop pleads guilty to falsifying warrant…,” “Police are ‘shielded’ from repercussions of their abuse,” and “As state-run police expand into Jackson… Others see racism”; 5) No balancing perspectives from police unions, rank-and-file officers, or critics of DOJ consent decrees are included in the author’s own framing, while the sole interviewee (the mayor) is aligned with reforms, producing a one-sided structural emphasis.
